paragraphs.body: The Use of Conflict to Propel Short Stories

Over the course of this class, I have read many short stories. Some I have seen before, and others were completely new. However, all of them showed extensive knowledge on how to move a story forward through dialogue and many other modalities, but for the sake of this paper, I will focus on conflict.

Selecting from the short stories we read in class, I have chosen Godmother Tea by Selena Anderson. Anderson writes, “I’d pass by the mirror and see fragments of other women… A blink of an eye revealed some dormant part of my personality, some no-longer-complete person who clutched her pearls at my audacity, the blasé way I naturally stood squandering my opportunities. Another quick glance revealed the godmother,” (pg. 3).

Joy is living a life that she doesn’t resonate with. Her apartment is filled with furniture brought over by her mother, when she herself would rather have it empty. When Joy’s ex-best friend, Nicole, is introduced, I could see the turmoil between what their relationship used to be and what it has turned into. “Across the room, Nicole was explaining a complicated point with her hands. When she caught me looking, she seemed annoyed, and I knew that if someone stormed in to shoot up the party as the blond person had prophesied, Nicole’s feelings toward me wouldn’t change,” (Anderson, pg. 8). It was mentioned by Anderson that Nicole and Joy had been best friends since childhood. However, Nicole has moved on from who she used to be, but Joy seems to be stuck molding herself into what everyone else wants to see.

annotations.comment: This is a nice discussion of one of the smaller conflicts in this story. I will say that I’m not entirely sure what you’re exploring about conflict at this point.
annotations.context_left: n she herself would rather have it empty. When Joy’s ex-best friend, Nicole, is introduced, I could
annotations.context_right: the turmoil between what their relationship used to be and what it has turned into. “Across the roo

paragraphs.body: When André is introduced, I learned even more about Joy through the way she would project herself over the phone to him. “I didn’t get the chance to beg him to come back to me, but I told some lies like I always do when I sense somebody doesn’t like me. I lied like I was genuinely happy at the simple accident of being alive. I talked until I felt like I was living in someone else’s idea,” (Anderson, pg. 10). Despite everything changing around Joy, she is still insistent on holding onto people who are no longer interested.

annotations.comment: Agree, but this does feel like characterization, not conflict.

paragraphs.body: At the very end, I thoroughly enjoyed the final confrontation with The Godmother. Throughout the story, Joy has seen her as harsh, due to the sharp words and criticisms thrown her way. But despite this verbal nature and rough exterior, The Godmother cooks for her, tells her the truth that she doesn’t want to hear, and forces Joy to confront the life that she is living. The Godmother says, “For example, the way you did your makeup today and the way you try to cook fancy dinners for yourself. None of that’s for the real you. You can’t even be real when nobody else is around,” (Anderson, pg. 16).

annotations.comment: Just FYI, you don’t need to cite, but it would be (Anderson 16) here. No need for the “pg”

paragraphs.body: I believe that Anderson’s story does a wonderful job at showing the external conflict of relationships changing but also highlights and focuses on the internal conflict that Joy is having. Despite the things around her, it is what Joy thinks of herself that is shaping how she behaves and thinks. The Godmother is just this internal thought and reflection being projected into something tangible. By making the Godmother real, Joy doesn’t have to confront herself directly about what she is doing but can rather believe that it is someone else telling it to her.

annotations.comment: Ah, okay, we focused on the thesis here.

paragraphs.body: The short story I selected that we did not read in class is “This is Pleasure” by Mary Gaitskill. This conflict is introduced very subtly from the very beginning. One of our main characters, Margot, describes a story told to her by Quin, where he makes sexual comments about a woman he met in the park. My favorite aspect of this story is the uncertainty between both Margot and Quin about the actions Quin has made and how each one feels about them. Gaitskill writes, “I laughed, but I wondered: Did the woman know, even dimly, that she was being toyed with? Did she feel that there was something wrong with the encounter, the way you might feel a mysterious hair drawn across your cheek? Why did I think it was so funny? It seems strange to me when I look back on it now. Because I don’t want to laugh. I feel pain. Real heart pain. Subtle. But real,” (pg. 134).

Quin refuses to believe that what he has done to these women over the years is even minorly impactful. He states, “… I believe that these emails are my best defense, even when they are a tiny bit sexy. Because they show mutuality, pleasure, even gratitude – friendship,” (Gaitskill, pg. 141).

Gaitskill also does a wonderful job at showing how the internal conflict of these characters can move through the story. Margot believed herself to be Quin’s friend, even though she herself was a victim to him. It is because of this friendship that she has a difficult time coming to terms with what Quin did and how she feels about it. Meanwhile, Quin is certain that this will breeze past him, and that what he did to those women was a mutual wanting, even if he held the position of power.

Within my own stories, I tried to put at least one element of internal conflict to show that it isn’t just about what is going on around my characters, but that their feelings and thoughts were just as important to move the stories along. I think that I achieved this best in my second short story, The Melody of Warfare. I wrote with the intention of my main character, Emrys, to be enveloped in the grief of losing his husband to the war that was occurring in their country. He is forced to confront this grief when he returns to their apartment in search of his husband, Malakai’s, violin. I wrote, “When he couldn’t take it anymore, he stumbled from the bedroom, breath coming faster than he could control. His eyes were so glassy that he couldn’t see where he was going, falling over the broken furniture and plaster.

His feet tripped out from under him, and he collided violently with the ground, pain bursting from his face as blood rose to the surface. Nothing could stop the panic that wracked his body. He trembled as everything that he tried to push down came flooding back.”

I feel as though the inner conflict of this story was portrayed well, although there were some areas that could be made stronger in order to help those who read it better understand the story and its importance. In both Anderson’s and Gaitskill’s stories, I as a reader not only understood the central conflicts but was also brought deeper into the world by gaining descriptions of where they were taking place, how the other characters were interacting with the main characters and seeing the overall theme the stories were trying to convey. I think that with some heavy revision, I could incorporate all of those things into my stories in order to strengthen them.

To quote Anne Lamott, “– and so you go along for the first six months thinking how nice life is now that God is there. Then you look out the window one day and see that there’s a wrecking ball outside. It turns out that God actually thinks your whole foundation is shot…,” (Bird by Bird, pg. 156). Although my story has all of the major things that it needs to function, it isn’t exactly built on the best foundation like some of the ones we read in class. In order for that to occur, I will most likely have to take them apart and put them back together to form something not necessarily better, but more to a higher standard.

annotations.comment: It would be better to pull a quote about conflict from Lamott.

paragraphs.body: I’ve learned a lot from the readings we did over the semester, and I hope to continue to learn how to improve my work as I continue on in the Creative Writing classes. Really breaking these stories down helped me to better understand where my stories may have been lacking in essential parts, which fixing, will allow me to become a better writer.

annotations.comment: I agree! I appreciate a lot of this paper, Emery, and yet I also found myself not quite sure what you’re arguing about conflict and how it ties to the craft Lamott discussed in Bird by Bird. If you want to try to tie this more tightly together, give it another draft and submit with your final portfolio.
